title: Sipping Tea with the LibrarAIn: How Cooperative AI Architecture Inverts the Cost Curve
thesis: Cooperative platforms can neutralise and ultimately reverse their structural AI cost disadvantage against venture-backed competitors by aggregating AI costs across membership â€” a 'Sipping Tea' model where each member adds a leaf to the shared pot, creating compounding institutional knowledge that becomes a permanent cooperative moat as the membership grows.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: A 7-section outline arguing that distributed cooperative AI cost aggregation creates a compounding advantage that venture-backed platforms cannot replicate.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: Innovations #1993 (Cooperative AI Cost Aggregation) and #1994 (Institutional Knowledge Compounding) anchor the paper. The core insight is that cooperative AI costs scale with membership (costs Ã· members â†’ lower per-capita as membership grows), while venture-backed AI costs scale against revenue (fixed VC runway depleted faster with growth). The 'Sipping Tea' metaphor captures the compounding dynamic: each member adds a leaf (small cost contribution), the shared pot grows richer over time, and the 'LibrarAIn' â€” the cooperative's AI as a librarian of collective institutional memory â€” becomes more capable and less expensive per member simultaneously.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The outline traverses seven sections: (1) the structural funding asymmetry between platform cooperatives and VC-backed competitors, framed as the AI 'death spiral' â€” cooperatives that cannot afford AI lose members to those that can; (2) the Sipping Tea aggregation model with explicit math (10,000 members Ã— $0.50/month = $5,000/month AI budget vs. finite VC runway); (3) institutional knowledge compounding â€” how every AI interaction in a cooperative becomes a member-owned asset that informs future AI, vs. the extractive platform where it becomes proprietary company data; (4) the cooperative AI architecture (shared inference pools, knowledge graph as cooperative asset [#2006], democratic AI governance, cost transparency); (5) the inversion point â€” the membership threshold at which cooperative AI becomes cheaper per capita than VC-backed AI, followed by a permanent compounding advantage; (6) the Liana Banyan case study (MoneyPenny shared receptionist, Contingency Operator, Cephas Knowledge Base, 27 production systems); and (7) implications, open questions, and future work.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Section 1 frames the death spiral through the lens of structural funding asymmetry: a cooperative cannot subsidise AI capabilities with investor capital, so it risks losing feature parity with VC-backed platforms. Section 2 introduces the aggregation arithmetic that breaks the spiral: if costs are distributed across the membership, each member's individual contribution is trivial while the collective budget is substantive. Section 3 argues the real moat is not cost parity but institutional knowledge compounding â€” the cooperative's AI learns from every member interaction, and that learning is a collective asset. Section 4 describes the architectural requirements: shared inference pools (batch queries to reduce per-call cost), bidirectional knowledge graphs (#2006), AI agents as cooperative employees (MoneyPenny, Pawn-style research assistants), cost transparency for member trust, and democratic AI prioritisation. Section 5 is the mathematical crux: the inversion point where cooperative AI becomes cheaper per capita than VC-backed AI, after which every new member reduces per-capita cost â€” the permanent compounding advantage. Sections 6-7 ground the theory in the Liana Banyan platform and identify open research questions (governance of AI priorities, privacy in collective knowledge, ownership of AI-generated insights).
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The paper's deepest theoretical contribution is the claim that institutional knowledge compounding is a qualitatively different competitive dynamic from standard network effects. Network effects (Metcalfe's Law: value scales as nÂ²) benefit all platforms proportionally to size. Institutional knowledge compounding is asymmetric: it benefits cooperatives more than extractive platforms because the knowledge stays with the collective rather than being captured by shareholders. In a venture-backed platform, AI training on user interactions produces IP that belongs to the company; if the company is acquired, the knowledge transfers to the acquirer. In a cooperative, the AI's institutional knowledge is a collective asset â€” the members cannot be acquired away from it. The LibrarAIn concept (MoneyPenny as institutional memory node) is the paper's empirical anchor for this claim: it is not an abstraction but a deployed system that screens contacts, remembers context, and learns preferences across the entire membership simultaneously. The Sipping Tea metaphor is precisely calibrated: 'sipping' connotes low individual cost (pennies per member per month), 'tea' connotes patience and steeping (the brew gets richer over time), and 'LibrarAIn' fuses the cooperative's AI role (librarian of collective memory) with the underlying technology (LLM). The outline's reference stack â€” Scholz (2016), Srnicek (2016), Zuboff (2019), Ostrom (1990), Bommasani et al. (2021) Foundation Models, and Cooperative AI Foundation working papers â€” positions the paper at the intersection of platform cooperativism and frontier AI governance scholarship. Status as outline: all seven sections are defined with bullet-level detail; the abstract is drafted; no section has been drafted to paragraph form.

composing_primitives: Democratic AI governance (member vote on AI budget priorities)
founder_voice_quotes: Each member adds a leaf; the shared pot gets richer for everyone.
The cooperative gets cheaper to run as it gets bigger â€” opposite of traditional platforms.
The longer the cooperative operates, the richer the brew.
Cooperative AI as competitive moat â€” not just cost parity, cost ADVANTAGE.
stratum_recommendation: Academic / White Paper; target venues include Journal of Cooperative Studies, tripleC, Cooperative AI Foundation workshop proceedings, or AAAI/NeurIPS cooperative AI workshops; also suitable for SSIR op-ed format

title: Four-Agent Architecture â€” V2 Literature Review Section (B061)
thesis: This literature review section for Paper V2 of the Four-Agent Architecture paper synthesises three intersecting scholarly domains â€” multi-agent AI governance, platform cooperativism, and AI adoption in SME/cooperative contexts â€” to establish the precise gap the paper addresses: no existing work specifies how role-specialised, human-governed AI agents should be architected, constrained, and coordinated inside a cooperative enterprise operating under non-extractive economic rules.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: A 44-source literature review section (B061, insert as Section 2) establishing the gap the Four-Agent Architecture paper fills across three scholarly domains.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: The review covers: (Â§2.1) multi-agent AI systems and governance â€” Wang et al. (2024) LLM agent survey, Wooldridge (2009) foundational ontology, Bai et al. (2022) Constitutional AI, Cardoso/Dignum/Vasconcelos (2025) normative multi-agent systems, the human-in/on/out-of-the-loop continuum literature, and Bianchi/MÃ¼ller/Ortega (2026) HLER as direct parallel to the Four-Agent division of labour; (Â§2.2) platform cooperativism â€” Scholz and Schneider (2016), Grohmann (2024) CoopCycle, Tortia (2023) cost-based pricing supporting Cost+20%, Gaba (2025) AI-driven cooperatives calling for 'detailed micro-architectures' without providing one; (Â§2.3) AI adoption in SME and cooperative contexts â€” OECD (2025) 11.9% SME AI adoption vs. ~40% large firms, G7 SME AI Adoption Blueprint, ILO/ICA/DAWI high-level principles without concrete architectures.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The gap statement (Â§2.4) is precise: existing scholarship either treats SMEs/cooperatives as AI adopters needing tools without addressing internal AI governance architectures, or theorises cooperative governance without specifying multi-agent AI system design. The Four-Agent Architecture intervenes at this intersection, providing an operational governance design that translates ILO/ICA democratic principles and OECD agentic-AI potential into a concrete, accountable multi-agent system for cooperative platform development. The 44 references are organised across three domain clusters with fully-formed academic citations, making this section insertion-ready for the paper with only formatting normalisation needed. This is a Section 2 insertion â€” existing sections 2â†’3, 3â†’4, etc., per the merge instruction.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Â§2.1 (Multi-Agent AI Systems and Human Governance): Moves from foundational ontology (Wooldridge 2009) through LLM-based agent surveys (Wang et al. 2024) to constitutional and normative constraints (Bai et al. 2022; Cardoso et al. 2025). The human-in/on/out-of-loop literature is characterised as a maturity continuum; HLER (Bianchi et al. 2026) is highlighted as the closest empirical parallel â€” specialised agents with human supervisory control for economic research. Enterprise architecture from AWS re:Invent (2025) and Mercer TAAP (2024, 2025) confirm role-based agent designs with escalation paths are standard in corporate deployments. The domain gap: all existing frameworks address corporate or enterprise contexts; none address cooperative multi-agent governance. Â§2.2 (Platform Cooperativism): From Scholz/Schneider manifesto to empirical analysis â€” Ghirlanda/Kirov (2024) SLR identifying barriers, Grohmann (2024) CoopCycle as strongest empirical case, Alvear/Rubim/Milanez (2026) Latin American federation. Tortia (2023) directly grounds Cost+20% floor pricing; Pattiniemi/Tainio (2024) address member-benefit pricing tension. Gaba (2025) is the paper's key gap citation â€” acknowledges AI-driven platform cooperatives but stops at conceptual level, calling for 'detailed micro-architectures.' Â§2.3 (SME/Cooperative AI Adoption): OECD 11.9% vs. 40% adoption gap, G7 Blueprint's 'dynamic self-sustaining ecosystems' frame, ILO/ICA/DAWI democratic technology governance principles â€” all prescribe without specifying. Â§2.4 consolidates into a two-sentence gap statement that positions the Four-Agent Architecture as the field-level intervention.
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The literature review performs three simultaneous operations. First, it legitimises the paper by demonstrating fluency across three distinct scholarly communities (AI systems, cooperative economics, SME policy) â€” a triangulation that signals to reviewers in any of the three domains that the paper is contextually grounded. Second, it constructs the gap not as an absence of interest but as a maturity asymmetry: all three domains have advanced independently (multi-agent AI to enterprise deployment, platform cooperativism to empirical case studies, SME AI policy to G7 ministerial statements) without their scholars engaging each other at the architectural specification level. Third, it positions the Four-Agent Architecture not as a theoretical contribution to any one domain but as a practical synthesis that is overdue given the maturity of all three. The Gaba (2025) citation is the review's rhetorical pivot â€” it acknowledges the need for 'detailed micro-architectures' while failing to provide one, making the Four-Agent Architecture the paper Gaba called for but did not write. The Tortia (2023) citation is the review's economic anchor â€” it is not asserted that cooperatives price at cost+margin; it is cited as peer-reviewed empirical finding. The 44-reference density is appropriate for a journal submission aiming at Annals of Public and Cooperative Economics, Journal of Cooperative Studies, or MIS Quarterly. Status: this is a single discrete section (Section 2) with full citations, ready for insertion into the larger paper. The broader paper's other sections are not present in this file.

founder_voice_quotes: No existing framework describes a multi-agent system designed to build and operate a cooperative platform under economic constraints, where the agents serve member interests rather than shareholder returns.

The Four-Agent Architecture intervenes at this intersection, providing an operational governance design that translates ILO/ICA democratic principles and OECD agentic-AI potential into a concrete, accountable multi-agent system.
stratum_recommendation: Insert as Section 2 of the Four-Agent Architecture paper (ai_cake anthology); target journals include Annals of Public and Cooperative Economics, Journal of Cooperative Studies, MIS Quarterly, or CSCW proceedings

title: Paper #7 â€” 'The $5 Career': Full Outline (B049)
thesis: A seven-section full outline (B049) for the $5 Career academic paper demonstrating that cooperative platforms can create entirely new economic roles at a $5/year entry cost by combining validated organic community demand, a diminishing-reward Pioneer program that solves the cold-start problem, and a multi-currency internal economy that reduces transaction costs to near-zero at scale â€” with Section 1 already drafted in the companion Hood Uber file.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: The master seven-section outline for Paper 7, defining all sections of the $5 Career paper with bullet-level detail and section-by-section word targets.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: Paper 7's seven sections cover: (1) the organic demand problem (Hood Uber 21,000 San Antonio participants as evidence), (2) the six Cue Card roles (Bounty Photographer, Pearl Diver, Home Teacher, Captain, Freezer Node Operator, Rideshare Driver), (3) the Pioneer Program (#2104) with diminishing-reward tiers solving the cold-start problem, (4) the four-currency subscription economy and its structural cost advantage, (5) comparative analysis vs. gig platforms (take-rate table with constitutional lock), (6) mathematical models (break-even at 500 members, profitability at 1,000), and (7) conclusions and policy implications. Target: ~10,000 words for SSIR or Journal of Cooperative Studies.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The outline is the architectural document for Paper 7. Section 1 status is 'DRAFTED' in the companion file (HOOD_UBER_SECTION). Sections 2-7 are outlined with three-to-five bullet sub-points per sub-section. The six Cue Card roles each have role-specific economics embedded in the outline: Bounty Photographer generates 110-240 Marks/month at zero storage cost to the platform; Pearl Diver can earn 833 Marks/month at 100 subscribers; Home Teacher earns $1,187-$2,400+/month at 11-20 hours/week with 83.3% retention vs. Udemy's 37%; Captain roles (#1975-#1978) include Walking Billboard, Tiered Commitment (C+20â†’C+90 mirroring military escalation of force), Apprentice system, and Geographic Corridor Campaign; Freezer Node Operator earns $800-1,500/month at 8-10 hours/week; Rideshare Driver integrates Hood Uber demand validation + Earn-Down vehicle ownership + Defense Klaus panic button. The Pioneer Program (Â§3) is framed as the cold-start solution: Founders' Circle (pioneers 1-10) receives 50 Marks/month + brass medallion + case study; rewards diminish through Trailblazer/Pathfinder/Early Adopter/Standard tiers until the role is statistically proven at 1,000 practitioners. The four-currency section (Â§4) quantifies the structural cost advantage: 60% internal / 40% dollar payment mix eliminates most Stripe fees and makes the cooperative cheaper to run as it scales. The comparative analysis (Â§5) includes a five-platform take-rate table with the constitutional lock (LB margin cannot increase without supermajority member-assembly) framed as 'enshittification-proof by design' (Doctorow). The mathematical models (Â§6) run break-even, marginal economics per role, internal economy growth projections (40% internal at 1K members â†’ 75% at 100K), and network effect scaling. Policy implications (Â§7) include cooperative insurance pooling as healthcare access, pioneer programs as workforce development without government subsidy, and internal currencies as community economic development tools.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: This is the master map of Paper 7. Section 1 (Hood Uber): documented organic demand, gap between platform extraction and informal absence, $5 entry point innovation. Section 2 (Six Roles): role architecture overview â€” Cue Card front/back format, shared infrastructure ('configuration, not code'), then role-by-role economic models with Marks/dollar income ranges, hours required, and platform cost. Section 3 (Pioneer Program): cold-start problem definition, TasteTester pattern applied to career creation, medallion-as-marketing-instrument, flywheel sequence (pioneer â†’ story â†’ 10 more â†’ statistics â†’ proven â†’ rewards unlock for next role). Section 4 (Four-Currency Economy): Marks/Credits/Joules/dollars mix, complete economic cycle example (Photographer earns Marks â†’ spends on Spanish class â†’ teacher earns Marks â†’ Pearl Diver â†’ HexIsle), effective platform cost dropping as internal economy grows, SEC defense via Innovation #1936. Section 5 (Comparative): take-rate table, constitutional lock, infrastructure sharing vs. individual burden. Section 6 (Math): break-even/profitability, role-by-role marginal economics (Photography $0 per claim, Teaching $0 video hosting), internal economy projections, network effects. Section 7 (Conclusions): what it demonstrates, policy implications, limitations, '$5 thesis' closing statement.
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The outline reveals Paper 7's structural argument as a three-layer proof. Layer 1 (empirical): Hood Uber demonstrates that demand for cooperative alternatives to extractive platforms is not theoretical but massive and spontaneous â€” 21,000 participants without marketing, safety infrastructure, or payment protection. This is the proof of problem. Layer 2 (architectural): The six Cue Card roles demonstrate that cooperative infrastructure can make these informal networks safe and sustainable at $5 entry cost, with role-specific economics that are superior to extractive platform alternatives on take rate (83.3% vs. Udemy 37%, Uber ~60%), constitutional protection (margin cannot increase), and infrastructure access (Defense Klaus, Earn-Down ownership, cooperative insurance pooling). This is the proof of solution. Layer 3 (mathematical): The break-even/profitability models, internal economy growth projections, and network effects demonstrate that the model is self-sustaining at achievable membership thresholds (500-1,000 in a locale) and improves structurally as it scales (transaction costs approach zero as internal economy dominates). This is the proof of viability. The Pioneer Program is the paper's most innovative mechanism â€” it solves the cold-start problem not with subsidies or venture capital but with reputational infrastructure (brass medallion, case study, attribution Marks) that converts proof into recruitment. The flywheel sequence is elegant: proof recruits, not theory. Version relationship: This outline (B049) is the earliest Paper 7 artifact. The Hood Uber Section (also B049) is the drafted form of Section 1. The V2 New Sections Draft (B082) adds three new sections that supplement but do not replace the B049 structure.

title: Paper #7 â€” 'The $5 Career': Section 1 Draft â€” Hood Uber as Organic Demand Evidence (B049)
thesis: The Hood Uber phenomenon â€” 21,000 San Antonio residents organising informal ridesharing through Facebook with no safety infrastructure, zero payment protection, and documented violence â€” provides three simultaneous validations for cooperative commerce: proof of massive organic demand for community-based alternatives, price sensitivity that confirms Cost+20% would be competitive, and pre-existing community trust that cooperatives need to formalise rather than build from scratch.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: Drafted Section 1 of Paper 7, establishing Hood Uber as empirical proof of organic demand for cooperative infrastructure.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: The four sub-sections establish: (Â§1.1) the consistent pattern of community self-organisation when platforms fail underserved populations â€” Hood Uber (San Antonio, March 2026, MySA/Martinez citation) as the most vivid recent example; (Â§1.2) the gap between Pole A (Uber/Lyft: 25-40% extraction, binary eligibility, surge pricing, contractor classification) and Pole B (informal networks: 0% platform extraction, 0% safety, documented violence including a driver shot over an unpaid fare) â€” the cooperative occupies the space between at Cost+20% with 83.3% driver retention; (Â§1.3) the $5 entry point innovation â€” $5/year membership transforms ridesharing from a capital-intensive gig-economy position (commercial insurance $150-300/month) into a cooperative career pathway with insurance pooling, tiered community-vouching background verification, and Earn-Down vehicle ownership; (Â§1.4) three validations: demand (21,000 participants zero marketing), price sensitivity (people accept personal risk to avoid Uber pricing), and community trust (participants trust unvetted strangers â†’ cooperative formalises existing trust rather than building it).
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The section is written in academic voice and is integration-ready for Paper 7. Citations are noted for insertion: MySA Martinez (March 28, 2026), Uber/Lyft take-rate public SEC 10-K filings, gig economy classification literature (Dubal, Rosenblat), cooperative economics (Birchall 2011, Hansmann 1996), platform cooperativism (Scholz 2016, Schneider 2018), and community self-organisation theory (Ostrom 1990). The most important argumentative move is Â§1.4's generalisation beyond ridesharing: every community with informal service networks (neighbourhood meal sharing, informal childcare cooperatives, unlicensed home repair networks) represents demand for the same pattern. This universalises the Hood Uber evidence from a transportation case study to a structural observation about informal cooperative self-organisation.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Â§1.1 establishes the Hood Uber phenomenon with specificity: San Antonio, 21,000 participants, Facebook organisation, no background verification, no payment protection, no insurance, documented violence (driver shot over unpaid fare), and the generalisation that identical informal networks exist in every major American city. Â§1.2 frames the gap analysis with precision: Pole A (Uber/Lyft) quantified at 25-40% extraction with binary background screening that permanently disqualifies nonviolent records; Pole B (Hood Uber) quantified at 0% platform extraction with enumerated risk externalities. The cooperative at Cost+20% occupies the middle: 83.3% driver retention plus safety infrastructure the informal network cannot provide. Â§1.3 focuses on the entry cost innovation: not the rideshare service itself (which existing platforms already offer) but the $5/year membership that makes career exploration survivable. The Earn-Down vehicle ownership model is the most structurally novel element â€” driving in a cooperative fleet vehicle with accumulated revenue eventually producing title transfer to the driver. Â§1.4 delivers the three validations with the explicit flag that they extend beyond ridesharing to all informal cooperative self-organisation.
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The section's most powerful rhetorical and evidentiary move is framing Hood Uber not as a failure to be corrected but as a success to be formalised. 21,000 participants without marketing, platform, or safety infrastructure is not evidence of dysfunction â€” it is evidence of extraordinary latent demand and pre-existing community trust. The cooperative does not need to build either. It needs to add: insurance pooling (so the driver shot over an unpaid fare has legal protection), tiered background verification with community vouching (so the community trust that already operates informally is formalised with due diligence), payment processing (so cash/P2P payment disputes cannot escalate to violence), and Earn-Down ownership (so the cooperative career creates equity, not perpetual dependence on the cooperative fleet). The $5/year entry cost is the paper's central innovation not because it is cheap but because it is survivable â€” a prospective rideshare driver in an underserved community does not need to risk $150-300/month commercial insurance to try the role. Failure costs $5. This is qualitatively different from any existing gig platform's onboarding calculus. Version relationship: This is the drafted form of Section 1 from the B049 full outline. It is complete and integration-ready. The B082 V2 New Sections Draft does not replace this section â€” it adds three new sections (Consensus Shift, Permanent Shock, Closet) that surround and supplement it in the V2 assembly.

composing_primitives: Hood Uber / informal rideshare network evidence
founder_voice_quotes: A cooperative that adds verified safety layers to this existing trust network doesn't need to BUILD trust â€” it needs to FORMALIZE trust that already exists.
The critical innovation is not the rideshare service itself, but the entry cost.

Every community with informal service networks represents demand for the same pattern: cooperative infrastructure that makes existing community self-organization safe and sustainable at $5 per career attempt.
stratum_recommendation: Integration-ready as Section 1 of Paper 7 full draft; no further outline work needed â€” draft is academic-voice complete pending citation insertion

title: Paper 6: Temporal Content Architecture â€” Scheduling as Infrastructure in Cooperative Platforms (B075)
thesis: Temporal Content Architecture (TCA) is a cooperative platform design pattern that inverts the operator monopoly on scheduling by distributing a single shared scheduling primitive across three user roles â€” operators (Staff Broadcast Schedule), creators (Cue Card Battery Dispatch), and consumers (Scheduled Viewing Beacons) â€” transforming content consumption from passive algorithmic feed-scrolling into deliberate appointment-based engagement.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: An 8-section outline for Paper 6, proposing that scheduling should be distributed as a cooperative primitive across operator, creator, and consumer roles using identical UI components.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: The core TCA argument: content platforms treat scheduling as an operator monopoly (the algorithm decides what you see and when); cooperative principles require distributing control; therefore, scheduling primitives â€” the SchedulingEntryBox component, RRULE/RFC 5545 recurrence rules, and calendar sync infrastructure â€” should be equally accessible to operators dispatching staff broadcasts, creators scheduling Cue Card Battery dispatches, and consumers setting Scheduled Viewing Beacons on their personal Helm calendars. The 'All the Pudding' TV Guide interface (with 'There is No Spoon' subtitle) provides temporal content discovery through Listings, Schedule (visual programming blocks), and Calendar (month-grid heatmap) views.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The 8-section outline covers: (1) the operator monopoly on time â€” how algorithmic feeds colonise attention, and the cooperative counter-pattern of distributing time as a resource; (2) related work across algorithmic feed research (TikTok FYP, Facebook News Feed, Netflix/Spotify recommenders), calendar systems and personal information management, DVR and programming interfaces (TiVo, YouTube Watch Later), deliberate practice and learning science (Ericsson, Gollwitzer implementation intentions, just-in-time learning), and the identified gap (no prior work on distributing scheduling primitives as a cooperative principle); (3) the Three-Surface Architecture â€” Surface 1 Staff Broadcast Schedule (operator role, Distribution Grid target, burst dispatching), Surface 2 Cue Card Battery Dispatch (creator role, member Helm Calendars, per-card timing and bounty integration), Surface 3 Scheduled Viewing Beacons (consumer role, personal Helm Calendar, recurrence and quiet hours); (4) shared primitive design â€” SchedulingEntryBox component, RRULE integration (RFC 5545), calendar sync layer; (5) 'All the Pudding' temporal content discovery â€” TV Guide metaphor, three viewing modes, Schedule Viewing bridge; (6) evaluation and research agenda â€” three quantitative hypotheses (beacon users show higher completion rates, deeper engagement, lower churn) and qualitative research plan; (7) discussion â€” implications, limitations (adoption friction, scheduling overhead, calendar fragmentation), generalisability; (8) conclusion â€” 'There is No Spoon' as design thesis: the constraint members experience is one they bend themselves, using a primitive equal to the one operators and creators use.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Section 1 frames the problem through algorithmic feed colonisation (Zuboff, Doctorow, Srnicek) and establishes the cooperative counter-pattern: if time is a resource, and cooperatives distribute resources, then scheduling control should be distributed. Section 2 surveys the four literature clusters: algorithmic feed research (TikTok, Facebook, Netflix/Spotify as scheduling systems), calendar and PIM research (Outlook/Google/Apple Calendar, notification systems, appointment psychology via Gollwitzer), DVR and programming interfaces (TiVo as first consumer scheduling primitive, YouTube Watch Later as 'read-later guilt accumulation,' Substack/RSS reader subscription inboxes), and deliberate practice/learning science (Ericsson deliberate practice, just-in-time learning, appointment-based vs. ambient consumption). Section 3 introduces the three surfaces and their shared primitive. Section 4 details the SchedulingEntryBox component (phone alarm clock visual design, field definitions, target parameter as context switch), RRULE/RFC 5545 integration, and calendar sync layer. Section 5 presents the All the Pudding TV Guide interface with its three viewing modes. Section 6 defines three testable hypotheses. Sections 7-8 discuss implications, limitations, and conclude with the 'There is No Spoon' design thesis. References noted include Zuboff (2019), Doctorow (2023), Srnicek (2017), Ericsson, Gollwitzer (1999), Fernandes et al. (2014), Scholz (2016), Schneider (2018), and RFC 5545.
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The paper's deepest contribution is the claim that scheduling is a dimension of platform power as significant as pricing or algorithmic curation, and that distributing scheduling control is therefore a cooperative principle equivalent in importance to distributing ownership. Doctorow's enshittification framework operates through algorithmic feed manipulation â€” platforms capture user attention by controlling the 'when' of content delivery. Zuboff's surveillance capitalism similarly depends on platforms owning the temporal dimension of user attention (knowing when users are susceptible to influence). By distributing the scheduling primitive to consumers through Viewing Beacons, the cooperative makes the algorithm's monopoly on 'when' structurally impossible â€” a member who has scheduled their viewing of a Cue Card for Tuesday at 7pm has exercised the same scheduling authority the platform operator exercises in dispatching the Staff Broadcast Schedule. The 'There is No Spoon' metaphor is precisely chosen: the constraint (the spoon â€” the algorithmic feed's control over what you see and when) is not real. When the cooperative gives members the scheduling primitive, the feed's monopoly dissolves. The paper's research agenda (Section 6) is measurable: beacon users should show higher completion rates, deeper engagement (time-on-content, re-reads), and lower churn than algorithmic-feed consumers. These are testable propositions that would ground the theoretical TCA claim in platform usage data. Status as outline: the abstract is complete; all sections defined with bullet-level detail; no section has been drafted to paragraph form. This is the sixth paper in the cooperative platform architecture series (Papers 1-5 companions: Lighthouse Ladder, Invisible Temperament, Self-Funding Economics, Portable Reputation, Contingency Operators).

There is No Spoon â€” the constraint is not real.
Time is a resource like any other â€” should it be distributed too?
Distributing primitives is a cooperative principle.
stratum_recommendation: Academic paper in cooperative platform architecture series (Papers 1-6); target CHI, CSCW, or Journal of Cooperative Studies; also suitable as companion to the Lighthouse Ladder and Invisible Temperament papers in the same series

title: Accounts Payable, Not Securities: The Legal Architecture of Eligible Marks in Cooperative Platform Economics (V1 â€” Bishop Session 027)
thesis: Liana Banyan's eligible Mark system â€” where members earn effort-debt Marks through labour and those Marks become deployable when the associated project receives funding â€” maps precisely to established accounts payable doctrine rather than securities law, failing all four prongs of the Howey test and mapping cleanly to UCC Article 9 receivables, IRC Section 451 constructive receipt, and a five-principle design framework for cooperative non-security internal currencies.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: First drafted version of the Accounts Payable paper (Bishop B027, March 2026), establishing the Howey four-prong failure analysis and the AP/UCC/IRC framework for eligible Marks.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: V1 covers four analyses: (1) Howey test â€” all four prongs fail (Marks are not purchased [Prong 1 fails], individually tracked not pooled [Prong 2 fails], provide wages/in-kind/governance not profit [Prong 3 fails], require members' own labour [Prong 4 fails]); (2) UCC Article 9 â€” eligible Marks as payment intangibles / receivables with assignment rights; (3) IRC Section 451 â€” constructive receipt at eligibility event, Substitution is 1099-reportable contractor compensation; (4) three structural safeguards (one-way valve, constitutional margin lock, Mark generation controls from differential only). Proposes five design principles for cooperative non-security internal currencies.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: V1 is a complete academic paper (~3,000 words) with abstract, six sections, references, and an author bio note crediting Innovation #1911 (Two-Domain Architecture). The Howey analysis is the paper's core: Â§2 walks through all four prongs with crisp rejection rationale for each. The AP framework in Â§3 provides a tabular mapping of AP accounting elements to eligible Mark equivalents (Vendor = Member, Invoice = Mark record, Payment terms = contingent on project funding, Triggering event = project funded, Payment = Substitution/Commission/Sponsorship). The UCC Article 9 analysis in Â§3.2 characterises eligible Marks as payment intangibles and Commission/Sponsorship deployment as receivables assignment under UCC Section 9-406. The IRC Section 451 analysis in Â§3.3 applies constructive receipt to the eligibility event. Â§4 covers three structural safeguards: the one-way valve (Two-Domain Architecture â€” no Creditsâ†’Cash pathway), constitutional margin lock (Cost+20% requires supermajority to change), and Mark generation controls (differential only, fully auditable provenance). Â§5 proposes five design principles: earn don't sell, track individually don't pool, compensate don't profit, require effort not investment, close the loop. Version relationship: V1 has the same core argument as V2 but lacks the Pawn Batch 12 legal validation (8 queries, 5 GREEN/3 YELLOW/0 RED), the Subchapter T Subchapter T patronage distribution framework (V1 uses IRC Â§451 constructive receipt instead), the ministerial/managerial distinction formalised by the March 2026 SEC-CFTC Joint Interpretive Release, the Daniel/Ripple precedents, the money transmission analysis (Texas Finance Code Â§152.152 closed-loop exemption, GENIUS Act, UCC Article 12), the supply-control four-layer architecture, the solvency covenant, and the seventh design principle (maintain solvency covenant). V1 is the foundational draft; V2 is the legally validated and expanded version.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Â§1 frames the cooperative currency dilemma: internal currencies need securities clearance because they are functionally necessary (local economic multipliers, WaterWheel recirculation) but occupy uncomfortable regulatory space. Â§1.2 describes the eligible Mark mechanism: Marks emerge from differential (work value minus compensation received), held ineligible until project is funded, then deployable through three channels. Â§2 executes the Howey four-prong analysis with crisp per-prong rejection. Â§3 maps eligible Marks to AP accounting elements and characterises them under UCC Article 9 (payment intangibles, receivables assignment) and IRC Â§451 (constructive receipt at eligibility, 1099-NEC/MISC at Substitution). Â§4 describes three structural safeguards. Â§5 proposes five design principles. Â§6 concludes: 'The law already has a framework for this. It's called accounts payable. We just needed to recognize the mapping.' References include Howey (1946), UCC Article 9, IRC Â§451, Jones (2026) WaterWheels paper, Motta/Ferreri (2018) complementary currencies, Stodder (2009), SEC Framework (April 2019), and Ostrom (1990).
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: V1 is a foundational legal argument paper, not a survey or review. Its contribution is framing â€” by naming eligible Marks as 'accounts payable' rather than 'tokens,' 'coins,' or 'credits,' it positions them within commercial law rather than securities law, and commercial law has decades of established precedent, UCC infrastructure, and IRS reporting frameworks that cooperatives can navigate without novel regulatory guidance. The paper's five design principles (Â§5) are the most durable output: they provide a template for any cooperative platform designing internal currencies, regardless of the specific currency mechanics. The concluding line ('The law already has a framework for this. It's called accounts payable. We just needed to recognize the mapping.') is the paper's Founder voice at its most concentrated. V1 vs. V2 relationship: V1 is more accessible (shorter, fewer legal frameworks, cleaner argument) but less legally fortified. V2 adds the Pawn Batch 12 validation, Subchapter T, ministerial/managerial distinction, Daniel/Ripple precedents, money transmission analysis, supply-control architecture, and solvency covenant. For academic publication, V2 is the submission-ready version. V1 remains useful as a practitioner summary or legal memo.

composing_primitives: Three Mark deployment channels: Substitution / Commission / Sponsorship
founder_voice_quotes: The law already has a framework for this. It's called accounts payable. We just needed to recognize the mapping.
Eligible Marks are accounts payable, not securities.
A member who works on a cooperative project has earned compensation. If the project is not yet funded, that compensation is contingent. When funding arrives, the compensation becomes payable.
FOR THE KEEP.
stratum_recommendation: Journal of Corporation Law, Stanford Social Innovation Review, or cooperative legal counsel memo; V1 is practitioner-accessible; V2 is the academic submission-ready version

title: Accounts Payable, Not Securities: The Legal Architecture of Eligible Marks in Cooperative Platform Economics (V2 â€” Bishop Session 028, Pawn Batch 12 Validated)
thesis: V2 of the Accounts Payable paper â€” incorporating Pawn Batch 12 legal validation across eight independent queries (5 GREEN / 3 YELLOW / 0 RED) â€” substantially expands the Howey defence with the Daniel/Ripple precedents and the March 2026 SEC-CFTC ministerial/managerial distinction, adds Subchapter T Subchapter T patronage distribution tax treatment (Form 1099-PATR), analyses money transmission risk and the Texas Finance Code closed-loop exemption, and formalises a four-layer supply-control architecture and constitutional solvency covenant as the complete seven-safeguard legal architecture for cooperative non-security internal currencies.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: Legally validated V2 of the Accounts Payable paper, expanded with Pawn Batch 12's eight-query validation and four additional legal frameworks not present in V1.
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: V2 adds four major frameworks to V1's foundation: (1) Howey Prong 1 strengthened by Teamsters v. Daniel (1979) â€” no voluntary investment decision â€” and SEC v. Ripple Labs (2023) â€” distributions to non-paying recipients; (2) Howey Prong 4 supplemented by the March 2026 SEC-CFTC Joint Interpretive Release formalising the ministerial/managerial distinction â€” all Mark lifecycle functions (issuance, eligibility determination, Substitution settlement, Commission deployment, SAA calculation) are ministerial (formula-driven, no discretion); (3) Subchapter T patronage distribution tax treatment replaces V1's IRC Â§451 constructive receipt as the primary tax framework, with Form 1099-PATR at the eligibility event; (4) money transmission analysis â€” Texas Finance Code Â§152.152 closed-loop stored value exemption, GENIUS Act inapplicability (two independent reasons), UCC Article 12 (Controllable Electronic Records) inapplicability (non-transferable Marks).
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: V2 retains V1's six-section structure and adds a Â§4.4 Supply Control Architecture, Â§4.5 Money Transmission Exemption, and Â§4.6 Solvency Covenant. The supply-control section describes four layers: formula-driven issuance at charter level (Mark Amount = Fair Market Value of Work âˆ’ Compensation Received), member-assembly adjustment bands (Â±15%), automatic throttle (issuance slows to 50% when outstanding Marks exceed 90% of delivery capacity), and prohibition of retroactive changes. The solvency covenant has four components: 90% cap on outstanding Marks, automatic throttle enforcement, breakage (2-3 year redemption window then cancellation) and forfeiture rules (90-180 day post-exit window for eligible Marks), and negative balance / insolvency treatment (Mark holders are unsecured creditors; the covenant maintains a buffer). The conclusion summarises eight legal dimensions in a GREEN/YELLOW status table: five GREEN (Prong 4 ministerial role, UCC Article 9 receivables, Subchapter T, money transmission closed-loop exemption, solvency covenant) and three YELLOW (Prong 1 one-way valve, supply control formula vs. discretion, insolvency treatment of unsecured creditors). Zero RED. The design principles are expanded from five (V1) to seven (V2) by adding: #6 Control supply by formula not discretion, and #7 Maintain a solvency covenant.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: The ministerial/managerial distinction (Â§2.4 V2) is the most consequential addition. The March 2026 SEC-CFTC Joint Interpretive Release formalises what the WIR Bank and TimeBanks USA precedents implied: if the cooperative's role in the currency lifecycle is purely ministerial (executing fixed rules without discretion), Howey Prong 4 fails because value does not derive from the promoter's managerial efforts. V2 provides a five-row table classifying each Mark lifecycle function as ministerial with rationale: issuance (formula-driven), eligibility (binary trigger â€” funded or not), Substitution settlement (1 Mark = $1 via LB Card, no negotiation), Commission deployment (member directs, cooperative executes), SAA calculation (formula-driven success multiplier). The Subchapter T section (Â§3.3 V2) replaces Â§3.3 V1's IRC Â§451 analysis. Under Subchapter T (IRC Â§Â§1381-1388), the cooperative deducts patronage distributions as qualified written notices of allocation. Eligible Marks satisfy all three criteria (state dollar amount, redeemable within 90 days via Substitution at member election, member consent via membership agreement). Income recognition occurs at the eligibility event (Step 2), not at deployment (Step 3) or settlement (Step 4) â€” supported by the Charley v. Commissioner (9th Cir. 1996) frequent-flyer miles analogy. The money transmission section (Â§4.5) analyses the closed-loop exemption across Texas Finance Code Â§152.152, nine Model Act states, GENIUS Act (inapplicable on two grounds: not fiat-pegged stablecoin, not transferable between persons), and UCC Article 12 (inapplicable: no transfer capability â†’ no 'control' in Article 12 sense). The critical prohibition is explicit: peer-to-peer Mark transfers are an instant MSB trigger; Liana Banyan prohibits them by architecture (no transfer function exists).
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: V2 is a qualitative leap from V1 in legal depth. The most important additions are the ministerial/managerial distinction and the supply-control architecture. The ministerial/managerial distinction resolves a latent vulnerability in V1: if the cooperative's board could exercise discretion over Mark supply â€” deciding to issue additional Marks outside the formula â€” Howey Prong 4 would be revived because the value of existing Marks would depend on the board's managerial decisions. V2's four-layer supply-control architecture eliminates this vulnerability at the charter level: the formula is charter-embedded, adjustment bands require member-assembly governance (not board discretion), the automatic throttle activates mechanically, and retroactive changes are prohibited. Board discretion over supply is named as a 'RED securities risk â€” the single most dangerous design choice a cooperative currency can make.' The solvency covenant is V2's other major contribution. A cooperative currency that over-issues is not just economically fragile â€” it is legally fragile. A post-insolvency court reviewing a failed cooperative currency might recharacterise it as speculative, unwinding the structural safeguards retroactively. The 90% delivery-capacity cap prevents this scenario. V2's version relationship to V1: V1 is the foundational argument; V2 is the legally validated and substantially expanded version. V2 is the submission-ready academic paper. V1 is better for practitioner summaries. The Pawn Batch 12 validation (8 queries, 5 GREEN/3 YELLOW/0 RED) is the most important methodological addition â€” it transforms the paper from a single-author legal argument into an independently validated legal architecture.

title: Structural Determinism in Cooperative Commerce: A Mathematical Framework for ROI Predictability
thesis: A cooperative commerce model incorporating three structural constraints â€” fixed margins (Cost+20%), pre-order demand validation, and distributed manufacturing with empirically-validated costs â€” reduces ROI variance from the Â±50-200% typical of traditional businesses to Â±5% bounded only by irreducible manufacturing tolerances, demonstrating mathematically that structural design can eliminate uncertainty endogenously rather than merely managing it exogenously.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: A January 2026 mathematical economics paper proving that Liana Banyan's three structural constraints reduce ROI variance to near-deterministic levels (~Â±5%).
skipping_stones_layers.more_details: The paper formalises 'structural determinism' â€” a business architecture where pricing, demand, and production cost variables are fixed by design rather than estimated by forecast. Three constraints drive the proof: (1) Cost+20% fixed margin (P = C Ã— 1.20) with invariant margin M = 0.20C; (2) pre-order demand validation that makes volume known, not estimated (V = V_preorder); (3) distributed manufacturing with empirically-validated costs from prototype development (C = C_empirical, CV â‰ˆ 3%). With a 50% production cost buffer providing >5Ïƒ coverage of cost overrun risk, the 95% confidence interval for a 1,000-unit order at $10.00 cost is $1,880-$2,120 â€” Â±3% of expected value, compared to traditional business variance of Â±50-200%.
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth: The paper's mathematical proof (Â§4) shows that under the three constraints, profit variance collapses to ÏƒÂ²(Ï€) = (0.20V)Â² Ã— ÏƒÂ²(C), with Ïƒ(C)/C â‰ˆ 0.03 from prototype data. The 50% production buffer (C_budgeted = 1.50 Ã— C_estimated) provides >5Ïƒ coverage of the 3% CV cost variance, effectively eliminating cost overrun risk. Empirical validation draws on: 170+ innovations with Fusion 360 CAD diagrams and physical prototypes; the HexIsle flagship product comparison table (traditional approach Â±100%+ variance vs. Liana Banyan approach Â±5%); and the Let's Make Bread distributed manufacturing nodes (<5% cost variance across node operators using standardised processes). Literature draws on Coase (1937) transaction cost economics, Ostrom (1990) cooperative commons management, Scholz (2016) and Schneider (2018) platform cooperativism, Mollick (2014) Kickstarter crowdfunding, Belleflamme et al. (2014) demand validation, Ulrich and Eppinger (2016) product design manufacturing variance, Ries (2011) lean startup, Zuboff (2019), and Srnicek (2017). Status: complete academic paper (~2,800 words), submitted for peer review consideration as of January 2026, the earliest of the paper series.
skipping_stones_layers.wading: Â§1 (Introduction): Frames the uncertainty problem in traditional business â€” compounding unknowns across demand, production costs, pricing, and market conditions. Argues that high ROI variance forces businesses to target inflated margins to achieve acceptable expected returns given the variance, producing the extraction economics of platform capitalism. Proposes 'structural determinism' as the alternative. Â§2 (Literature Review): Transaction cost economics (Coase 1937, Williamson 1979/1985); cooperative economics (Ostrom 1990, Scholz 2016, Schneider 2018 â€” identifies 'predictable returns within cooperative models' as underdeveloped theoretical gap); pre-order/crowdfunding (Mollick 2014, Belleflamme et al. 2014). Â§3 (Liana Banyan Model): Three structural constraints with formulas; additional risk mitigation (50% buffer, distributed node architecture, variable member-operator labour costs). Â§4 (Mathematical Proof): Profit function Ï€ = 0.20VC; variance analysis showing collapse to (0.20V)Â² Ã— ÏƒÂ²(C); residual cost variance (Ïƒ(C)/C â‰ˆ 0.03); 95% CI calculation for 1,000-unit $10.00-cost product: $1,880-$2,120. Â§5 (Empirical Validation): 170+ innovations with prototype data; HexIsle case study; Let's Make Bread distributed manufacturing nodes. Â§6 (Limitations): Force majeure, pre-order cancellation (2-5%), scaling assumptions for novel manufacturing, cooperative governance stability. Â§7 (Discussion): Implications for cooperative economics (equitable outcomes AND predictable returns â€” not a tradeoff), platform design (structural uncertainty elimination allows lower margins without lower expected returns), and impact investing (below-market variance with market-rate returns while maintaining mission alignment). Â§8 (Conclusion): 'The structure is the strategy. When the structure guarantees the economics, the economics become predictable.' Appendix: notation summary.
skipping_stones_layers.diving_in: Full Pudding-tier: The paper's deepest intellectual contribution is the inversion of the standard transaction cost economics application. Coase (1937) and Williamson (1985) argue that organisations exist to minimise transaction costs â€” they accept uncertainty as exogenous and choose organisational form to manage it. The Liana Banyan model inverts this: organisational design can reduce uncertainty endogenously, making it unnecessary to build elaborate governance structures to manage what cannot be eliminated. This is not merely a cost-efficiency argument; it is an architectural argument about what business models can be. The impact investing implication (Â§7.3) is the paper's most consequential for capital formation: mission-driven capital that currently accepts below-market returns or higher variance 'as the cost of social impact' can recalibrate once structural determinism is recognised. The tradeoff is not impact vs. return but structural constraint vs. optionality. An investor who demands pricing flexibility (the right to raise margins when market conditions allow) accepts higher variance in exchange for that optionality. Liana Banyan's model eliminates optionality structurally (Cost+20% is constitutional) and in exchange produces Â±5% variance rather than Â±50-200%. For a patient impact investor, this is a superior risk-adjusted position even if the expected return is identical â€” lower variance at equal expected return is, by definition, a better investment. The paper's January 2026 date makes it the earliest of the series â€” it precedes the Accounts Payable papers (March 2026), the Five Dollar Career outline (B049), and the other academic papers in the series. It is the mathematical foundation that the later papers build on: the Self-Funding Economics paper (Papers 1-5 series) is presumably the more developed successor, but this paper provides the formal proof that self-funding economics are not aspirational but mathematically demonstrable.
